Select Board Work Session - June 16, 2026

Select Board · Brookline · 2026-06-16
tap to load the tape · nothing plays until you do
AI summary — supplements the official record

During the Select Board Work Session on June 16, 2026, the board discussed various topics, including the postponement of agenda item 1B for further discussion [01:26]. They reviewed the minutes from the previous meeting and registered multiple committee vacancies, notably in the Disability Commission [17:40]. There was discourse on the need for a new advisory committee to enhance civic engagement and public input in decision-making [198:15]. Additionally, the board addressed funding concerns related to the affordable housing trust fund and the potential unencumbering of $3.75 million [115:55]. The session included a focus on infrastructure needs across various departments and collaboration between the town's boards and residents for future zoning and development projects. The meeting ultimately concluded without any formal votes being recorded.

45:17 What about the Human Resources Board?

45:24 So yes, I have

45:26 I

45:27 put it this way.

45:30 A lot of

45:32 communities have moved away from being

45:33 resources to boards. The Human Resources

45:35 Board was created in

45:38 in in the '40s

45:41 back when the town was run primarily by

45:44 volunteers.

45:45 And they handled a lot of the HR

45:48 function of the town. And over time as

45:50 the town became more professionalized,

45:52 fewer and fewer functions were made

45:54 Human Resources to them. But it's still

45:56 featured by laws, and it does two it

45:59 does serve two important functions. One,

46:01 it

46:03 reviews job new job descriptions or new

46:05 job reclassifications and determines

46:07 whether or not they're appropriate

46:09 before we move on to before they are

46:11 allowed to be posted. And the other

46:13 thing it does is it services an

46:14 intermediary hearings that when there

46:17 are grievances filed by a union.

46:20 And that's built into all of the union

46:22 contracts. So that in step two of the

46:24 grievance process is the human resource

46:26 department. Now,

46:28 one of the reasons why I

46:31 hoped that we

46:33 were going to be able to talk about

46:34 moving the HR board because we were

46:35 having trouble filling that position.

46:37 They have to be residents of Brookline

46:39 and have to have HR experience, but they

46:41 can't be conflicted out. So often times

46:43 we would say, "Oh, hey, you're a labor

46:44 lawyer." And they'd say, "Yeah, well, I

46:46 may be representing someone who has an

46:48 interest, and therefore [clears throat]

46:49 I can't take this job." So it was very

46:51 hard to find recruits. We were down, we

46:53 were we were at bare minimum for quite a

46:56 while, and we were about to lose it.

46:57 Thankfully, someone did step up, so it's

46:59 no longer as big of an issue this year.

47:01 But it is still something I would like

47:03 us to see look at. But what I learned in

47:06 so doing is that really the way that

47:09 that's going to have to happen is it's

47:10 going to have to come through union

47:12 negotiations first. Um the

47:14 we're going to go through collective

47:15 bargaining. We're in collective

47:17 bargaining now with FAST, and that's

47:18 progressing. We will be back in

47:20 collective bargaining when these expire

47:22 at the end of all of the meetings for me

47:24 in in the next calendar year because FY

47:28 they're

47:29 sorry.

47:30 Um these expire these agreements expire

47:32 at the end of FY '27. So

47:35 once it is bargained, once we bargain

47:38 that,

47:39 um it'll change. Um

47:42 it would become effective on passage of

47:43 a bylaw revising it. And so I think

47:46 that's the order of operations we would

47:48 need to go through.

54:10 um we built a charge

54:27 and we had a good discussion and I think

54:39 the board at the time and sense of the

54:41 board was we don't want to create a

55:03 want to necessarily create that

55:06 investing the authority even though the

1:33:01 So

1:33:02 it has always been an open question in

1:33:04 this town as whether or not once you

1:33:05 appoint someone you have the ability to

1:33:07 remove them.

1:33:08 Uh

1:33:10 My take on it has been the power to

1:33:13 appoint usually inherently includes the

1:33:15 power to remove. Not everyone sees it

1:33:16 that way.

1:33:18 Uh

1:33:18 there is no specific language in in the

1:33:21 bylaws or the town administrator act

1:33:23 that controls my job vis-a-vis your jobs

1:33:27 that says if you appoint someone you

1:33:29 also

1:33:30 have the right to remove.

1:33:31 Um so

1:33:34 with that said

1:33:36 absence

1:33:38 is a

1:33:40 this language was intended to create a

1:33:41 way in which absence would chronic

1:33:43 absence here is basically be taken as a

1:33:45 de facto resignation.

1:33:48 If you are not doing the job of the

1:33:50 committee and you don't show up to the

1:33:52 meetings then you are presumed to have

1:33:55 resigned there.

1:33:57 And in order to make that as clear as

1:33:59 possible, we would have to go on record

1:34:01 and say, "Hey, you've been absent for

1:34:03 putting such a time. Can you please step

1:34:05 down?"

1:34:07 If that person does step down,

1:34:10 then if there's a well-documented

1:34:12 history of Basically, if you have proof,

1:34:14 you can say, "Yeah, I know this person

1:34:15 really has been a no-show and not." Or

1:34:17 there's a concern that the work of the

1:34:18 committee might not be done otherwise.

2:03:10 comes before that the those orange

2:03:12 rectangles are

2:03:14 years where construction

2:03:17 happened. And the yellow is the

2:03:19 permitting. That's both permitting and

2:03:21 planning board and ZBA, but also

2:03:22 building permitting.

2:03:24 And there is usually a year between the

2:03:26 ZBA permit and the money pulling the

2:03:28 building permit cuz they have to get the

2:03:30 construction documents and that kind of

2:03:31 thing. So this is just a graphic showing

2:03:33 all of

2:03:35 the things that have to happen before

2:03:37 that yellow permitting

2:03:39 color and pink here are the number of

2:03:41 years that each of the projects took for

2:03:44 pre-zoning work. No, how to get the

2:03:46 zoning in place.

2:03:48 And

2:03:50 there's been different economic cycles

2:03:52 in in Brookline.

2:03:54 We're not seeing a lot of cranes in the

2:03:55 whole Boston metro area right now, which

2:03:57 is

2:03:58 not surprising.

2:03:59 But up until COVID, you can see kind of

2:04:02 a steady march of pre-zoning work,

2:04:04 permitting, and then new construction

2:04:06 coming online until we hit COVID. And I

2:04:09 don't think it was just because of

2:04:10 COVID, but we also had

2:04:13 at least but we had two

2:04:16 big development zoning efforts that did

2:04:19 not have enough support to move forward.

2:04:21 And those are kind of the middle here.

2:04:22 You can see those white boxes

2:04:25 coming after that, right? That nothing

2:04:26 came of that those efforts. One is the

2:04:29 Lower Boylston

2:04:31 rezoning work, and the other one is

2:04:32 Temple Place,

2:04:34 which for a hot second Um, we were

2:04:36 thinking maybe lab use. In the long run,

2:04:39 I'm very glad that it didn't go

2:04:41 anywhere, that we didn't carry out here

2:04:42 in the buying place,

2:04:44 um, because that same developer is, um,

2:04:46 looking at doing housing,

2:04:48 um, so they're likely to lost commercial

2:04:49 space in the development, you know, so

2:04:51 hopefully for housing over there.

2:04:53 So,

2:04:54 um, that is that. Moving forward, some

2:04:57 of the work that was done, like with the

2:04:59 adjustments to the, um,

2:05:06 fruition. Um,

2:05:08 Claremont

2:05:09 has submitted an application or

2:05:11 pre-application, um,

2:05:13 for housing with, um, the next couple of

2:05:18 see in the River Road development plan

2:05:19 out. So, that permitting is started, the

2:05:21 zoning way. There's also larger projects

2:05:24 that you all on the select board may not

2:05:27 neighborhood, but things like, uh, 40

2:05:31 permitting for a couple years now. It

2:05:33 didn't require zoning to move forward,

2:05:36 but did require some variances. Is that

2:05:38 right?

2:05:38 Yes, variances.

2:05:43 Yes, and they did also change the

2:05:45 project, um,

2:05:47 kind of at a late stage to provide more,

2:05:51 um,

2:05:51 small units.

2:05:54 So, that's coming online, and then, um,

2:06:01 the talks, filling in, setting up

2:06:07 kind of, um, pattern coming back again

2:06:50 the

2:06:51 um, efficient use of resources? What you

2:06:53 know, how are we going to get the most

2:06:59 department-wide, not just

2:07:04 you can make this. So, at the time,

2:07:07 right,

2:07:08 the Brookline Housing Authority wanted

2:07:09 high-density development because we

2:07:11 rezoned that as part of the MBTA work.

2:07:17 They saved millions of dollars compared

2:07:21 history.

2:07:22 Um, that was both you know, well

2:07:24 community supported as well as a brand

2:07:25 new development on the St. Mary's Street

2:07:27 line. And then on the lower end of the

2:07:29 spectrum, um, at the time, uh, broadly

2:07:32 expanding accessory dwelling units. At

2:07:34 that time, there wasn't a lot of

2:07:35 community support to do that. Um, and it

2:07:38 it would have um,

2:07:40 not been a very efficient use of

2:07:41 resources because of community support

2:07:43 display. So, that's just a couple years

2:07:45 ago.

4:20:24 Um, we have historically interpreted it

4:20:26 to mean the local population. Um,

4:20:28 we've tried to, you know,

4:20:30 we we know that our demographic makeup

4:20:34 is different than other demographic

4:20:35 makeups, but we also know that, you

4:20:38 know, to a point that I think one of our

4:20:39 outreach is, you know, one reason why

4:20:41 people of color don't come to Brookline

4:20:44 is because they view Brookline as

4:20:46 historically hostile to their

4:20:49 um their communities. And so, we you

4:20:53 know, just to take one example, and so

4:20:55 we realize that ensuring that people

4:21:00 able to be

4:21:02 um

4:21:02 and and feel confident that that this is

4:21:04 a community that welcomes and encourages

4:21:07 participation from everybody is

4:21:08 important. So, it's not just oh, there

4:21:12 minority around, so we're not going to

4:21:13 put them on we're not going to recommend

4:21:14 them for boards or commissions. We don't

4:21:16 have a

4:21:17 quota system or anything like that.

4:21:20 concerned about how we define

4:21:43 that. And of course, we want to be

4:21:47 little worried about tracking it solely

4:21:50 based on the local population.

4:21:52 I think we can we can and that's a

4:21:54 that's a good
